text: The surgeon gains access through open laparoscopy in the left upper quadrant and places two 5mm ports on the left side. Adhesions are identified and divided. The stoma is examined, revealing the parastomal hernia with a visible defect on the lateral aspect of the stoma.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the stoma and passes it through the central hole of a DynaMesh IPST, a three-dimensional pre-shaped composite mesh. The mesh, measuring fifteen by fifteen centimeters, features a central hole with a two-centimeter cuff, allowing the stoma to be positioned with sufficient length to fashion a spout.
The surgeon introduces both the mesh and the intestine through the wound, positioning the mesh intraperitoneally with its longitudinal axis oriented upside down to facilitate stretching.

The surgeon fixes the mesh in an onlay position using stacks arranged in a double crown pattern, beginning with the outer crown. External pressure is applied to secure the mesh, and the intraperitoneal pressure is reduced to twelve millimeters of mercury to aid in the fixation process.
The surgeon unfashions the stoma and secures it to the skin. The excess intestine is repositioned into the abdomen, and the mesh cuff is adjusted around the stoma.

The surgeon applies the remaining inner crown of tacks to secure the mesh fixation in the abdomen. The mesh is inspected and appears to be in a good position. The abdomen is then deflated, and the port site is closed.
